(II and (VA or VB).)

(II and (VA or VB).)
I&C SCI 33.  Intermediate Programming.  4 Units.
Intermediate-level language features and programming concepts for larger, more complex, higher-quality software. Functional programming, name spaces, modules, class protocols, inheritance, iterators, generators, operator overloading, reflection. Analysis of time and space efficiency.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 32 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
(II and VB)
I&C SCI 45C.  Programming in C/C++ as a Second Language.  4 Units.

(II and VB)
I&C SCI 45C.  Programming in C/C++ as a Second Language.  4 Units.
An introduction to the lexical, syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic characteristics of the C/C++ languages for experienced programmers. Emphasis on object-oriented programming, using standard libraries, and programming with manual garbage collection.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 with a minimum grade of C or EECS 40 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
I&C SCI 45J.  Programming in Java as a Second Language.  4 Units.

I&C SCI 45J.  Programming in Java as a Second Language.  4 Units.
An introduction to the lexical, syntactic, semantic, and pragmatic characteristics of the Java language for experienced programmers. Emphasis on object-oriented programming, using standard libraries, and programming with automatic garbage collection.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 with a minimum grade of C or CSE 43 with a minimum grade of C.
Overlaps with I&C SCI 22, I&C SCI 23, CSE 23.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment. I&C SCI 45J may not be taken for credit after I&C SCI 22, I&C SCI 23, or CSE 23.
I&C SCI 46.  Data Structure Implementation and Analysis.  4 Units.

I&C SCI 46.  Data Structure Implementation and Analysis.  4 Units.
Focuses on implementation and mathematical analysis of fundamental data structures and algorithms. Covers storage allocation and memory management techniques.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 with a minimum grade of C and I&C SCI 45C with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
(Vb)
I&C SCI 51.  Introductory Computer Organization.  6 Units.
Multilevel view, design, and operation of computer system components. Machine-level data and instruction representation. Instruction sets and addressing modes. Memory organization. Laboratory work using low-level programming languages.

Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 and I&C SCI 6B.
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
(II)
I&C SCI 53.  Principles in System Design.  6 Units.
Introduces basic principles of system software: operating systems, compilers, and networking. Exposure to the following topics through theoretical and practical programming experiences: linking and loading, process and memory management, concurrency and synchronization, network communication, programming for performance, etc.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 46 with a minimum grade of C and I&C SCI 51 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.

I&C SCI 60.  Games and Society.  4 Units.
The study and critical analysis of computer games as art objects, cultural artifacts, gateways to virtual worlds, educational aids, and tools for persuasion and social change. Emphasis on understanding games in their historical and cultural context.
Same as GDIM 41
Restrictions: Computer Game Science majors and Game Design and Interactive Media majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of ICS majors have second right of consideration.
(III)
I&C SCI 61.  Game Design Fundamentals.  4 Units.

(III)
I&C SCI 61.  Game Design Fundamentals.  4 Units.
Explore the fundamentals of game design through the design and play of simple analog game systems. Students explore the principles and usage of game design elements through hands-on projects emphasizing iteration and playtesting in a creative game design community.
Same as GDIM 25
Restrictions: Computer Game Science majors and Game Design and Interactive Media majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of ICS majors have second right of consideration
(II)
I&C SCI 62.  Game Technologies and Interactive Media.  4 Units.
Technologies for interactive media and game design. Web-based software systems, virtual world platforms, and game engines. Emphasis is on conceptual and architectural aspects of these technologies.

I&C SCI 80.  Special Topics in Information and Computer Science.  2-4 Units.
Studies in selected areas of information and computer sciences. Topics addressed vary each quarter.
Prerequisite: Prerequisites vary.
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times as topics vary
I&C SCI 90.  New Students Seminar.  1 Unit.
Introduces students to the Donald Bren School of Information and Computer Sciences. Focuses on advising students making the transition to UCI, community building, and mostly surveying the technical areas within departments in ICS, via talks by faculty on their research.
Grading Option: Pass/Not Pass only
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.

I&C SCI 139W.  Critical Writing on Information Technology.  4 Units.
Study and practice of critical writing and oral communication as it applies to information technology. Each student writes assignments of varying lengths, totaling at least 4,000 words.
Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of the Lower-Division Writing requirement.
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
(Ib)
I&C SCI 161.  Game Engine Lab.  4 Units.
The use of an open source game or graphics engine in the design and implementation of a computer game. Principles of game engine design. Students work on teams to design, implement, and evaluate new computer games based on an engine.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 45C or GDIM 32 or I&C SCI 167.

Prerequisite: I&C SCI 45C or GDIM 32 or I&C SCI 167.
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
I&C SCI 162.  Modeling and World Building.  4 Units.
Use of 3D modeling software and related tools to design and create animated, textured models, and expansive virtual worlds incorporating objects, scenes, and venues for activity within game worlds and online environments.
Corequisite: I&C SCI 61 and GDIM 25 may also be taken as corequisites.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 and (I&C SCI 6N or MATH 3A) and (I&C SCI 61 or GDIM 25).
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
I&C SCI 163.  Mobile and Ubiquitous Games.  4 Units.

I&C SCI 163.  Mobile and Ubiquitous Games.  4 Units.
Design and technology of mobile games, including mixed reality gaming, urban games, and locative media. Case studies of significant systems. Uses and limitations of location-based technologies. Infrastructures and their relationships to gameplay and design.
Prerequisite: (I&C SCI 61 or GDIM 25) and (GDIM 31 or I&C SCI 10 or I&C SCI 31 or I&C SCI H32).
I&C SCI 166.  Game Design.  4 Units.
Game design takes into consideration psychology, narrative, platform features and limitations, marketing, computer science capabilities, human-computer interface principles, industry trends, aesthetic judgment, and other factors. Students focus on video game design through lectures, readings, presentations, implementation, and play testing.

Prerequisite: I&C SCI 61 or GDIM 25.
Restrictions: Computer Game Science majors, Game Design and Interactive Media majors, and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment. School of ICS majors have second consideration for enrollment.
I&C SCI 167.  Multiplayer Game Systems.  4 Units.
Foundations and technologies that enable multiuser, networked, and persistent virtual environments. Emphasis on database design and management, network protocols, and concurrency control to accommodate large numbers of simultaneous users.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 51 or GDIM 31.
Restrictions: Computer Game Science majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.

I&C SCI 168.  Multiplayer Game Project.  4 Units.
Design and develop a multiplayer game using Unity or similar engine, with a focus on learning to work in a development team, writing design documents, working with platform features and limitations, developing engaging mechanics, playtesting, and post-mortems.
Prerequisite: (GDIM 27 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 166 with a minimum grade of C) and (GDIM 33 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 161 with a minimum grade of C) and (GDIM 61 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 62 with a minimum grade of C).
Same as GDIM 161
Restrictions: Computer Game Science majors and Game Design and Interactive Media majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of ICS majors have second consideration for enrollment.

I&C SCI 169A.  Capstone Game Project I.  4 Units.
Students work in teams to design and implement a unique game or interactive experience. Emphasis on sound, art, and level design, building a community, production values, full utilization of hardware and software platform, and current industry trends.
Prerequisite: GDIM 27 with a minimum grade of C or GDIM 61 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 62 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 166 with a minimum grade of C.
Same as GDIM 167A
Restrictions: Seniors, Computer Game Science majors, and Game Design and Interactive Media majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of ICS majors given second right of consideration.
I&C SCI 169B.  Capstone Game Project II.  4 Units.

I&C SCI 169B.  Capstone Game Project II.  4 Units.
Students continue work in teams to design and implement a unique game or interactive experience. Emphasis on sound, art, and level design, building a community, production values, full utilization of hardware and software platform, and current industry trends.
Prerequisite: GDIM 167A with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 169A with a minimum grade of C. In Progress grade for GDIM 167A or I&C SCI 169A also accepted.
Same as GDIM 167B
Restrictions: Seniors, Computer Game Science majors, and Game Design and Interactive Media majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of ICS majors given second right of consideration.
I&C SCI 192.  Industrial or Public Sector Field Study.  2-4 Units.

I&C SCI 192.  Industrial or Public Sector Field Study.  2-4 Units.
Students participate in an off-campus, supervised internship for a minimum of 60 hours. Students apply classroom knowledge through internship projects in the private sector or nonprofit agencies.

Repeatability: May be taken for credit 4 times
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
I&C SCI H197.  Honors Seminar.  1-2 Units.
An overview of computer science and selected recent trends in research. Students attend talks on current faculty research, with opportunities for discussion.
Grading Option: Pass/Not Pass only
Repeatability: May be taken for credit for 4 units

Grading Option: Pass/Not Pass only
Repeatability: May be taken for credit for 4 units

Theories, methods, and resources for teaching computer science at the university level, particularly by teaching assistants. Classroom presentations, working with individuals, grading, motivating students. Participants will give and critique presentations and may be videotaped while teaching.
Grading Option: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory only

Informatics (IN4MATX)
Courses
IN4MATX 43.  Introduction to Software Engineering.  4 Units.
Concepts, methods, and current practice of software engineering. Large-scale software production, software life cycle models, principles and techniques for each stage of development.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 32 or I&C SCI H32.
Overlaps with I&C SCI 105.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 80.  Special Topics in Informatics.  4 Units.
Designed for informatics instructors to pilot new teaching materials and prototype new class. The format of the class is flexible based on the content to be offered in the future.
Prerequisite: Prerequisites vary.

Prerequisite: Prerequisites vary.
Repeatability: May be taken 3 times as topics vary
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX H81.  Ethics, Technology, and Design.  4 Units.
Provides a critical framework for how and why biases of many kinds are built into everyday digital tools. Reflections on ethics, technology, and design through case studies drawn from machine learning, CS education, engineering, social media, and criminal justice.
Restrictions: Campuswide Honors Collegium only.
(III)
IN4MATX 101.  Concepts in Programming Languages.  4 Units.

(III)
IN4MATX 101.  Concepts in Programming Languages.  4 Units.
In-depth study of several contemporary programming languages stressing variety in data structures, operations, notation, and control. Examination of different programming paradigms, such as logic programming, functional programming and object-oriented programming; implementation strategies, programming environments, and programming style.

Same as COMPSCI 141
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students only.
IN4MATX 113.  Requirements Analysis and Engineering.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 113.  Requirements Analysis and Engineering.  4 Units.
Equips students to develop techniques of software-intensive systems through successful requirements analysis techniques and requirements engineering. Students learn systematic process of developing requirements through cooperative problem analysis, representation, and validation.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 with a minimum grade of C and IN4MATX 43 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 115.  Software Testing, Analysis, and Quality Assurance.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 115.  Software Testing, Analysis, and Quality Assurance.  4 Units.
Preparation for developing high-quality software through successful veriﬁcation and validation techniques. Fundamental principles of software testing, implementing software testing practices, ensuring the thoroughness of testing to gain conﬁdence in the correctness of the software.
Prerequisite: (I&C SCI 45J with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 45C with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 46 with a minimum grade of C) and IN4MATX 43 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 117.  Project in Software System Design.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 117.  Project in Software System Design.  4 Units.
Specification, design, construction, testing, and documentation of a complete software system. Special emphasis on the need for and use of teamwork, careful planning, and other techniques for working with large systems.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 43 and I&C SCI 33.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 119.  Engineering Artificial Intelligence Software.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 119.  Engineering Artificial Intelligence Software.  4 Units.
Design, deploy, and manage software incorporating AI components in real-world production environments. Topics include types of models, in-house vs. third-party models, retrieval augmented generation, fine-tuning, prompt engineering, model testing, and reliability.
Prerequisite: (I&C SCI 45C with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 45J with a minimum grade of C) and (STATS 7 or STATS 67).
Restrictions: Informatics majors and Software Engineering majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of Information and Computer Sciences students only. Computer Science Engineering Majors have second consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 121.  Software Design: Applications.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 121.  Software Design: Applications.  4 Units.
Introduction to application design: designing the overall functionality of a software application. Topics include general design theory, software design theory, and software architecture. Includes practice in designing and case studies of existing designs.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 33 with a minimum grade of C or GDIM 33 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 122.  Software Design: Structure and Implementation.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 122.  Software Design: Structure and Implementation.  4 Units.
Introduction to implementation design: designing the internals of a software application. Topics include design aesthetics, design implementation, design recovery, design patterns, and component reuse. Includes practice in designing and case studies of existing designs.
Prerequisite: (I&C SCI 45J or I&C SCI 46) and (IN4MATX 101 or COMPSCI 141).
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 124.  Internet Applications Engineering.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 124.  Internet Applications Engineering.  4 Units.
Concepts in Internet applications engineering with emphasis on the Web. Peer-to-Peer and Interoperability. Topics include HTTP and REST, Remote Procedure/Method Calls, Web Services, data representations, content distribution networks, identity management, relevant W3C/IETF standards, and relevant new large-scale computing styles.
Prerequisite: (COMPSCI 132 or EECS 148) and I&C SCI 45J.
Same as COMPSCI 137
Overlaps with COMPSCI 122B.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 125.  Computer Game Development.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 125.  Computer Game Development.  4 Units.
Introduction to the principles of interactive 2D and 3D computer game development. Concepts in computer graphics, algorithms, software engineering, art and graphics, music and sound, story analysis, and artificial intelligence are presented and are the basis for student work.
Prerequisite: COMPSCI 112 or COMPSCI 171 or IN4MATX 121 or ART 106B or I&C SCI 163 or I&C SCI 166.
Same as COMPSCI 113
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 131.  Human Computer Interaction.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 131.  Human Computer Interaction.  4 Units.
Basic principles of human-computer interaction (HCI). Introduces students to user interface design techniques, design guidelines, and usability testing. Students gain the ability to design and evaluate user interfaces and become familiar with some of the outstanding research problems in HCI.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 10 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 31 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 32A with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C or GDIM 31 with a minimum grade of C or ENGR 10 with a minimum grade of C or ENGRMAE 10 with a minimum grade of C or EECS 10 with a minimum grade of C.

Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 132.  Advanced Topics in Human-Computer Interaction.  4 Units.
Advanced material in the space of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) through application and practice. Introduces user research methods such as observation, interviewing, usability testing, design activities, and user analytics.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 131.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 133.  User Interaction Software.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 133.  User Interaction Software.  4 Units.
Introduction to human-computer interaction programming. Emphasis on current tools, standards, methodologies for implementing effective interaction designs. Widget toolkits, Web interface programming, geo-spatial and map interfaces, mobile phone interfaces.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 45J with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 45C with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Computer Science and Engineering majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 134.  Advanced Topics in User Interface Software.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 134.  Advanced Topics in User Interface Software.  4 Units.
Advanced material in the space of human-computer interaction programming. Introduces core interface toolkit concepts including interface states, event handling, layout and positioning, and custom interactions. Materials Fee
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 131 and IN4MATX 133.
Restrictions: Informatics majors, Software Engineering majors, and Business Information Management majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of Information and Computer Sciences students only. Computer Science Engineering Majors have second consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 141.  Information Retrieval.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 141.  Information Retrieval.  4 Units.
An introduction to information retrieval including indexing, retrieval, classifying, and clustering text and multimedia documents.
Prerequisite: (I&C SCI 45C with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 45J with a minimum grade of C) and (STATS 7 or STATS 67 or AP Statistics with a minimum score of 3).
Same as COMPSCI 121
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 143.  Information Visualization.  4 Units.
Introduction to interactive visual interfaces for large datasets, and to principles of human visual perception and human computer interaction that inform their design. Various applications for data analysis and monitoring are discussed.

Prerequisite: (IN4MATX 131 with a minimum grade of C or IN4MATX 43 with a minimum grade of C) and (I&C SCI 31 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C).
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 148.  Designing for the Internet of Things.  4 Units.
Introduction to design principles for Internet of Things applications. Prototyping, design, and evaluation of physical computing applications, smart environments, embedded systems, and future computing scenarios. Includes hands-on in-class laboratory exercises. Materials Fee

Prerequisite: I&C SCI 10 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 31 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 32A with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.
IN4MATX 151.  Project Management.  4 Units.
Introduces theoretical and practical aspects of project management. Topics include organizational theory, group behavior, project management skills, case studies, personal and group productivity tools, management of distributed work, stakeholders, consultants, and knowledge management. Students do a project exercise.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 43 or GDIM 61.
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.

IN4MATX 153.  Design of Social and Interpersonal Technologies.  4 Units.
Considerations around the design of technologies beyond the individual. Topics may include social media, messaging and communication, feeds, algorithmic recommendations, privacy, and access.
Prerequisite: (IN4MATX 161 or IN4MATX 43) and (I&C SCI 31 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C).
Restrictions: Informatics majors, Software Engineering majors, and Business Information Management majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of Information and Computer Sciences students only.
IN4MATX 161.  Social Analysis of Computing.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 161.  Social Analysis of Computing.  4 Units.
Examines current computing technologies and their consequences at the individual, group, community, and societal level. Topics may include privacy, trust, identity, reputation, governance, ethics, social justice, and culture.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 10 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 31 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 32A with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C or ENGR 10 with a minimum grade of C or EECS 10 with a minimum grade of C or ENGRMAE 10 with a minimum grade of C. Satisfactory completion of the Lower-Division Writing requirement.
Restrictions: School of Information and Computer Sciences students have the first consideration for enrollment.

IN4MATX 162.  Information Systems in Organizations.  4 Units.
Introduction to role of information systems in organizations, components and structure of organizational information systems, and techniques used in information systems analysis, design, and implementation.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 10 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI 31 with a minimum grade of C or I&C SCI H32 with a minimum grade of C or GDIM 31 with a minimum grade of C.
Restrictions: Informatics majors and Software Engineering majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of Information and Computer Sciences students only.
IN4MATX 163.  Project in Organization Analysis of Information Systems.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 163.  Project in Organization Analysis of Information Systems.  4 Units.
Students undertake a project in analyzing the use of information systems in organizations. Introduces research methods such as observation, interviewing, and document analysis. Students gain experience with techniques including jotting, writing field notes, qualitative coding, and memoing.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 162 or IN4MATX 162W.
Restrictions: Informatics majors, Software Engineering majors, and Business Information Management majors have the first consideration for enrollment. School of Information and Computer Sciences students only.
IN4MATX 164.  Children's Learning and Media.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 164.  Children's Learning and Media.  4 Units.
Examines how popular media may impact how young people learn, develop, and communicate by looking at research related to the impacts of a wide range of popular media including television, video games, digital environments, mobile devices, and other multimedia.

Same as EDUC 130

IN4MATX 171.  Introduction to Health Informatics.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 171.  Introduction to Health Informatics.  4 Units.
Broad overview of medical informatics for students with varied backgrounds. Electronic medical records, online resources, mobile technologies, patient safety, and computational design. Legal, ethical, and public policy issues. Health systems management. Evaluation and fieldwork for health systems.
Prerequisite: WRITING 60 with a minimum grade of C or WRITING 30 with a minimum grade of C or WRITING 31 with a minimum grade of C or HUMAN 1C with a minimum grade of C or HUMAN H1C with a minimum grade of C.
Same as PUBHLTH 105
Restrictions: Satisfactory completion of the Lower-Division Writing requirement.
IN4MATX 172.  Project in Health Informatics.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 172.  Project in Health Informatics.  4 Units.
Students undertake significant quarter-long projects related to health informatics. Topics may include field evaluations of health care technologies, prototypes, iterative design, and system implementations.

Same as PUBHLTH 106
IN4MATX 173.  Consumer Health Informatics.  4 Units.
Consumer health informatics is a subfield of health informatics aimed to empower healthcare consumers through a variety of consumer-facing technologies. Covers health behavior change, health literacy, social support, and the design, evaluation, and implementation of major technological applications.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 171 with a minimum grade of C or PUBHLTH 105 with a minimum grade of C.

The increasing availability of electronic heath data provides an unprecedented opportunity to use data-driven approaches to improve human health. Equips students with essential skills in curating, managing, and analyzing health data, focusing on electronic health records.

Same as PUBHLTH 110

Group supervised project in which students analyze, specify, design, construct, evaluate, and adapt a significant information processing system. Topics include team management, professional ethics, and systems analysis.

IN4MATX 191B.  Senior Design Project.  4 Units.
Group supervised project in which students analyze, specify, design, construct, evaluate, and adapt a significant information processing system. Topics include team management, professional ethics, and systems analysis.

Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of the Lower-Division Writing requirement.

Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of the Lower-Division Writing requirement.

Individual research or investigation under the direction of an individual faculty member.

IN4MATX 201.  Research Methodology for Informatics.  4 Units.
Introduction to strategies and idioms of research in Informatics. Includes examination of issues in scientific inquiry, qualitative and quantitative methods, and research design. Both classic texts and contemporary research literature are read and analyzed.

IN4MATX 203.  Qualitative Research Methods in Information Systems.  4 Units.
Introduction to qualitative research methods used to study computerization and information systems, such as open-ended interviewing, participant observation, and ethnography. Studies of the methods in practice through examination of research literature.

IN4MATX 205.  Quantitative Research Methods in Information Systems.  4 Units.
Quantitative research methods used to study computerization and information systems. Design of instruments, sampling, sample sizes, and data analysis. Validity and reliability. Longitudinal versus cross-sectional designs. Analysis of secondary data. Studies of the methods through examination of research literature.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 251 with a minimum grade of B- or IN4MATX 261 with a minimum grade of B-. Basic knowledge of elementary statistics is also required.
IN4MATX 207S.  Doctoral Seminar on Research and Writing.  2 Units.

IN4MATX 207S.  Doctoral Seminar on Research and Writing.  2 Units.
Doctoral seminar centered on original research and writing. Provides a chance for doctoral students at all levels to present original work, brainstorm ongoing issues, and learn to provide and receive critical feedback from peers.
Grading Option: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory only
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times
IN4MATX 209S.  Seminar in Informatics.  2 Units.
Current research and research trends in informatics. Forum for presentation and criticism by students of research work in progress.
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times as topics vary
IN4MATX 231.  User Interface Design and Evaluation.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 231.  User Interface Design and Evaluation.  4 Units.
Introduction to the design and evaluation of user interfaces, with an emphasis on methodology. Cognitive principles, design life cycle, on-line and off-line prototyping techniques. Toolkits and architectures for interactive systems. Evaluation techniques, including heuristic and laboratory methods.
IN4MATX 232.  Research in Human-Centered Computing.  4 Units.
Introduction to contemporary topics in human-computer interaction, including methods, technologies, design, and evaluation. Emerging application domains and their challenges to traditional research methods. Advanced architectures and technologies. Critical issues.
Prerequisite: Some familiarity with HCI principles.

Prerequisite: Some familiarity with HCI principles.
IN4MATX 241.  Introduction to Ubiquitous Computing.  4 Units.
The "disappearing computer" paradigm. Differences to the desktop computing model: applications, interaction in augmented environments, security, alternate media, small operating systems, sensors, and embedded systems design. Evaluation by project work and class participation.
Same as COMPSCI 248A
IN4MATX 244.  Introduction to Embedded and Ubiquitous Systems.  4 Units.

Same as COMPSCI 248A
IN4MATX 244.  Introduction to Embedded and Ubiquitous Systems.  4 Units.
Embedded and ubiquitous system technologies including processors, DSP, memory, and software. System interfacing basics; communication strategies; sensors and actuators, mobile and wireless technology. Using pre-designed hardware and software components. Design case studies in wireless, multimedia, and/or networking domains.
Prerequisite: I&C SCI 51 and COMPSCI 152 and COMPSCI 161 and (I&C SCI 6N or MATH 3A or MATH 6G or I&C SCI 6D). B.S. degree in Computer Science is also accepted.
Same as COMPSCI 244
IN4MATX 251.  Computer-Supported Cooperative Work.  4 Units.

Same as COMPSCI 244
IN4MATX 251.  Computer-Supported Cooperative Work.  4 Units.
The role of information systems in supporting work in groups and organizations. Examines various technologies designed to support communication, information sharing, and coordination. Focuses on behavioral and social aspects of designing and using group support technologies.
IN4MATX 261.  Social Analysis of Computing.  4 Units.
The social and economic impacts of computing and information technologies on groups, organizations, and society. Topics include computerization and changes in the character of work, social control and privacy, electronic communities, and risks of safety-critical systems to people.
IN4MATX 263.  Computerization, Work, and Organizations.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 263.  Computerization, Work, and Organizations.  4 Units.
Selected topics in the influence of computerization and information systems in transforming work and organizations. Theories of organization and organizational change. Processes by which diverse information technologies influence changes in work and organizations over short and long time periods.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 251 with a minimum grade of B- or IN4MATX 261 with a minimum grade of B-.
IN4MATX 265.  Theories of Information Society .  4 Units.

IN4MATX 265.  Theories of Information Society .  4 Units.
Social and economic conceptions of information technology. Macrosocial and economic conditions that foster changes in information technologies. Social construction of information and computer technology in professional worlds. Theories of information technology and large-scale social change.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 251 with a minimum grade of B- or IN4MATX 261 with a minimum grade of B-.
IN4MATX 267.  Digital Media and Society.  4 Units.

IN4MATX 267.  Digital Media and Society.  4 Units.
Selected topics in the technological and social aspects of online interactions, and policy including online games, social media, electronic activism, e-commerce, and digital libraries. Media-theoretic approaches to digital technology. Architectures, infrastructure considerations, and their consequences.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 251 or IN4MATX 261.
IN4MATX 273.  Information Technology in Global Sustainability.  4 Units.
Explores the relationship between recent developments in information technology and the global transition to sustainability. Topics include the role of IT systems in the provision of human needs and wants (e.g., smart grids, food systems, and other IT-enabled infrastructure).

IN4MATX 280.  Overview of Human-Computer Interaction and Design .  4 Units.
Introduction to human-computer interaction and user-centered design. The material is focused on laying the groundwork for understanding the history, importance, and methods of human-computer interaction and design.
Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.
IN4MATX 281.  User Needs Analysis .  4 Units.
Understanding the user's context, needs, and preferences. Topics include interviews and observations, modeling the context, flow, culture, space, and artifacts involved in an endeavor, ways of aggregating what is found, and presenting these findings to others.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 280 with a minimum grade of B-.
Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.

Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.
IN4MATX 282.  Design and Prototyping .  4 Units.
Introduction to user-centered design and prototyping. Focused on practical methods for interaction design. Topics include the nature of design and the challenges to creating and evaluating good designs, as well specific skills for designing interactive systems.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 280 with a minimum grade of B-.
IN4MATX 283.  User Experience Evaluation .  4 Units.
Evaluating prototypes and completed systems. Topics include comparative analysis, laboratory experiments, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthroughs, surveys, clickstreams, and help-desk.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 280 with a minimum grade of B-.
Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.

Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.
IN4MATX 284.  Advanced Design and Prototyping .  4 Units.
Develop and communicate interactive technology design prototypes. Moving concepts from brainstorming and paper prototypes to wireframe and limited functionality mock-ups.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 282 with a minimum grade of B-.
Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.
IN4MATX 285.  Interactive Technology Studio .  4 Units.
Technologies, languages, and skills required for creating prototypes to communicate interactive technology concepts. Topics include HTTP, CSS, CSS scripting, AJAX, Design Patterns, Javascript, Javascript libraries such as jQuery, SQL, MVC, and cloud architectures.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 280 with a minimum grade of B-.

Prerequisite: IN4MATX 280 with a minimum grade of B-.
Restrictions: Master of Human Computer Interaction and Design only.
IN4MATX 286.  Innovations in HCI and Design .  4 Units.
Recent social and technological developments in human-computer interaction and design. Topics will vary as the field progresses but include novel input techniques, novel platforms, and innovations in theory and methods of design.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 280 with a minimum grade of B-.
IN4MATX 287.  Capstone Project in HCI and Design .  4 Units.
Group project that reinforces all concepts learned in this program, including knowing where user experience work is most appropriate and essential, and executing the appropriate steps.

Prerequisite: IN4MATX 283 with a minimum grade of B- and IN4MATX 284 with a minimum grade of B-.
Restrictions: Human Computer Interaction and Design majors only.
IN4MATX 288.  Capstone Project and Portfolio .  4 Units.
Completion of capstone projects and development of portfolios. Ideation, critique, development, and critique.
Prerequisite: IN4MATX 287 with a minimum grade of B-.
IN4MATX 289.  Special Topics in Applying Human-Computer Interaction and Design in Industry.  4 Units.
Selected topics related to understanding and applying human-computer interaction and design concepts and practices within the User Experience industry. Topics addressed vary each quarter.
Grading Option: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory only
Repeatability: May be taken 4 times as topics vary

Grading Option: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory only
Repeatability: May be taken 4 times as topics vary
Restrictions: Human Computer Interaction and Design majors and School of Information and Computer Sciences students only.
IN4MATX 290.  Research Seminar.  2 Units.
Forum for presentation and criticism by students of research work in progress. Presentation of problem areas and related work. Specific goals and progress of research.
Grading Option: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory only
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times
IN4MATX 295.  Special Topics in Informatics.  4 Units.
Studies in selected areas of informatics. Topics addressed vary each quarter.
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times as topics vary
IN4MATX 298.  Thesis Supervision.  2-12 Units.

IN4MATX 298.  Thesis Supervision.  2-12 Units.
Individual research or investigation conducted in preparation for the M.S. thesis option or the dissertation requirements for the Ph.D. program.
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times
IN4MATX 299.  Individual Study.  1-12 Units.
Individual research or investigation under the direction of an individual faculty member.
Repeatability: May be taken unlimited times

You need to be able to code in Python for CS 178. But it's mostly theoretical, with one assignment where you implement gradient descent

CS 122A (Intro Databases)- super useful, important for almost all fields in CS so should be a must-take. best profs are carey (retiring next spring) and wongma. Mostly theory/design until week 7, but all important.
CS 121(Information Retrieval) - very interesting and useful class where you build a search engine from scratch. all profs solid, I personally like martins most. Highly recommend.
CS 178 (ML and Datamining)- great if you want a solid foundation in machine learning. nice curve, hard content and tests, homework isnt too bad. Overall good, would recommend, especially if you are interested in ML. Profs solid, I like mandt.
CS 190 (medical DL) - very easy. nice way to get into research, cool content.

Now for stuff I plan to take:
CS 143A (Operating Systems) and CS 142A (Compilers) - idealy with harder prof. often considered a must-take CS course.

CS 162 (computation theory) - if your interested in theory

Graduating CS undergrad here, inspired primarily by this post. Here's a comprehensive review of every undergraduate CS elective I took, just to help guide students through the process. A lot of the UCI CS hivemind likes to advertise courses such as CS 121 and CS 142A, but I tried to just follow my interests.

There are a lot of underrated classes that just never fill up at UCI, and I think a lot of them deserve more attention.

Note that this is coming from the perspective of a prospective graduate student and that my reviews are mostly specific to the quarter I took them in. My course opinions are mostly specific to my own interests, which are more geared toward theoretical computer science. Know that YMMV.

I typically evaluate courses on two primary criteria: how much I learned during the course and how engaging it was. I typically do not care for grades/GPA boosters, but if there is a notably unfavorable grade distribution for a course (>15% Ds or Fs, or <25% A- or higher), I will point it out.

Hope this helps. If needed, I can elaborate more on my experiences.
Name	Instructor	Recommend?	Notes

CS 122A - Introduction to Data Management	Nawab	If you intend to take CS 122B or CS 122C, sure. Otherwise, maybe not worth your time. It's not a bad course, but I reckon there's better options.	Not my cup of tea, but I know a lot of students find this to be an essential course to take. This class gives a brief introduction into the theory behind database design, focuses on how to write a SQL query, and ends on optimization techniques. The content in this course is good to know if you will be working with databases in the future. Lectures can be slow and awkwardly paced, and I found myself a little bored with how superficial some of the content was. It's not a bad course by any means, and it gets the job done, but it does leave me wanting a lot more than what I actually got out of it.

CS 122B - Project in Databases and Web Applications	Herold	If you like web development or intend to do industry work as a software engineer, this is a definite recommend. Otherwise, make sure you know what you're getting yourself into.	You spend 10 weeks creating Fabflix, which is effectively just IMDb from Temu — it’s a website where users can browse for movies and purchase them. You slowly build up all the small components over time with a partner, then record a video demo to showcase the functionality of the website. There is a key focus on keeping your website secure and scalable. Have some fun with it — you should definitely be doing more than the bare minimum. However, note that this course is undeniably heavy, and you should not go into it without having coordinated with a

this course is undeniably heavy, and you should not go into it without having coordinated with a potential partner to work on this project with. Completing this project, in my opinion, is a massive accomplishment.

CS 141 - Concepts in Programming Languages	Klefstad	Depends on what you want out of the course. If you want a soft introduction to modern programming languages, it accomplishes the job perfectly. Otherwise, you might be disappointed.	This course, much like every other Klefstad course, has a lot of busywork and can be stressful to manage with all the constant deadlines. A lot of these assignments, in my opinion, don't accomplish that much, but if you stay on top of things, you shouldn't get overwhelmed. Content-wise, it’s pretty fun and interesting — you go over several principles of programming languages and the questions that shape their designs. It's a lot of breadth and not a lot of depth, so if you wanted an authentic course on programming language theory, I don't think this is the

so if you wanted an authentic course on programming language theory, I don't think this is the class for you. The highlight of this class is the extra credit assignment. If you're taking this course out of interest, please do this. It will expand your horizons coding up a past assignment in a new programming language, as you'll get to see how different languages are suited for different purposes, which is, in effect, one of the main takeaways of this course anyway. Note that there is a bare minimum performance expected of you on the quizzes and final to pass, regardless of your current grade in the course. Be wary of this.

CS 142A - Compilers and Interpreters	Demsky	If you're really into compilers, petition to skip this course and take CS 142B. This course's sluggish nature and oversimplified toy project will likely not satisfy your thirst. If you're just looking to dip your toes, this course does the job.	The first half of the course is pretty standard, and in my opinion, kind of uninteresting. The latter half of the course, which is entirely code generation, is where the course starts getting interesting. This is when you actually learn about ways to optimize code by removing dead code, reusing values as much as possible, and replacing variables with constants whenever possible. Then, you translate all that into assembly and allocate registers. When you think about what compilers do, this is probably what

into assembly and allocate registers. When you think about what compilers do, this is probably what you think of. Unfortunately, you do none of that optimization in this course’s project, which comprises most of your grade. In my opinion, that's a lot of wasted potential to learn about this content, have the perfect opportunity to apply it... and then just not. Lectures are for the most part kind of unengaging, and after a few weeks or so, he'll just be lecturing to a room with only 15 people in it. If you have high expectations for this course, probably damper them.

CS 162 - Formal Languages and Automata	Shindler	Absolutely. If you're looking for the science part of Computer Science, I strongly recommend that every CS major take it.	 I think this course is so incredibly fundamental that I would recommend that everyone in CS take it. Computational complexity theory is just so all-encompassing in the field that I really think you’d be missing out on a good chunk of CS if you skipped this course. You go over NFAs, PDAs, recognizability, NP-completeness, etc. A good chunk of this class is dedicated to proof-writing, so be wary of that. However, every proof has an established formula to it, so you don't have to worry too much. Just make sure you practice outside of class.

CS 165 - Project in Algorithms and Data Structures	Goodrich	Yes, especially if you're into algorithms.	A really nice "survey course" about the topic of experimental analysis. Your main goals during the projects are to implement algorithms, benchmark them, and then write a report about your findings. If you're really into the "translating theoretical ideas into practice" space, I think that you'll get quite a lot out of this course. If you like algorithms as a field, definitely take this.

CS 166 - Quantum Computation and Information	Fukuzawa	Yes.	The first third of the course is on fundamentals, the second third dives deeper into the principles of quantum information, and the final third is on the actual algorithms. The topics can definitely be intimidating, especially if your linear algebra background isn’t great. However, I think what really makes this course stand out is the ability for the instructors to break down complicated topics into digestible formats. If you have any interest in this topic, you will like the course. Strongly recommend if you want to step outside your von Neumann architecture comfort zone.

CS 171 - Introduction to Artificial Intelligence	Kask	If you intend to take CS 175 or CS 179, sure. Otherwise, maybe not worth your time. It's not a bad course, but I reckon there's better options.	Man was this course disorganized. Course is very outdated, with the projects being up in Python 3.5 or something like that. I would not recommend this course on that basis alone if not for the fact that the content, although superficial, is incredibly important to the field of AI. However, there is considerable overlap with other courses you might have already taken or will take, which kind of diminishes the identity of this course even further. Lectures are rather unengaging, but whatever. The project is probably the best part for learning. Do more than the bare minimum on it, experiment with
is probably the best part for learning. Do more than the bare minimum on it, experiment with strategies to get the best performance.

CS 177 - Applications of Probability in Computer Science	Sudderth	Must take. Nearly every other course I've made the "Yes" recommendation on the condition that you are interested in the content of the course. I think every CS major should take this course, period. The content is way too useful and applicable to miss out on.	Despite being in the CS 17X series, this class isn't just for the AI/ML enthusiasts. I think it's for everyone in CS --- there are applications to computer graphics, algorithm analysis, and discrete processes. You learn about a topic (such as a continuous distribution) and then it’s immediately followed up by an application of said topic. The homeworks are primarily application-focused. I honestly believe you don’t need much of a background from STATS 67 to succeed

I honestly believe you don’t need much of a background from STATS 67 to succeed here, as long as you have at least rudimentary familiarity with the content. Overall, it’s a really great course that opens your eyes to the vast possibilities of probability applications.

CS 178 - Machine Learning and Data-Mining	Ihler	Must take for anyone interested in AI/ML.	An impressive and comprehensive introduction to the field of machine learning. The content of this course is extremely dense, but Ihler will distill all that content down to its bare basics when it comes time to do homeworks and exams. Despite this, the exams are still quite difficult, so be warned. The course typically introduces the concepts of machine learning to you right out of the gate, then you spend the next several weeks diving deeper into several models that can be trained on the data sets, as well as tricks to make them perform better. This is an intimidating course, but it will truly broaden your perspectives by the end of it — you will learn a lot. Make sure you have a strong background

your perspectives by the end of it — you will learn a lot. Make sure you have a strong background in calculus, linear algebra, and statistics. You don't need them to get a good grade, but you will need them if you want to actually understand what's happening.

CS 179 - Algorithms for Probabilistic and Deterministic Graphical Models	Ihler	Must take for anyone interested in AI/ML.	This course effectively takes the Bayesian networks and Markov chains you were introduced to in CS 171… then cranks them up to 11. Probabilistic graph models are quite huge for simulation and inference. Monte Carlo methods, Ising machines, and Hidden Markov Models are all quite fundamental to the field of AI. This is about as close to a graduate course as you’re getting with undergraduate courses. Be warned that the content is not trivial and that you’ll probably need at least some extensive math background to understand the inner workings of some of the things he’s talking about. However, you will learn a lot in this course if you put the effort in to follow along.
about. However, you will learn a lot in this course if you put the effort in to follow along. Don't worry about your grade in this course --- it's basically a free A. Focus on the content.

45C makes more sense for next quarter, since you'll get to have Professor Thornton. He's the best teacher for 45C, hands down. Plus, if your goal is to learn, you want Wong-ma for 51 and 53, and she's not teaching the class next quarter.

But numbers don't really matter for classes to the extent you might be thinking. 45C doesn't have to be before 51, although it probably should (because knowing some C++ is beneficial, compared to not knowing any C-languages at all).

If you want winter to be easy, load up in fall. Take ICS 46 and 51 and Informatics 43, and just accept it'll be a lot of work for eleven weeks. Then you can have your GE, writing, and Anthro 2A in Winter, a nice chill schedule

How is the workload for ICS 46 and 51 combined? I've heard 46 tends to be the hardest lower-div and 51 with Wong-ma isn't easy either. I don't mind switching Informatics 43 to Winter and replacing it with literally the easiest ge I can find or a random 2 unit filler.

They are very doable together. Just remember to go to class, stay on top of the work, and start your assignments when they're assigned, not just before they're due. Both classes have projects that can be stress-free enough if you give yourself plenty of time.

I took ICS 46 (Shindler) and ICS 51 (Wong-ma) together with a language course as my third class. Didn't face any difficulties (ended with A's in all three), but that may have been because 46 with Shindler was pretty easy

Top schedule is more balanced, and better since its best to get the general series (31-32-33-45c-46) done. I can break them down if you wanna read.

Summer 2022:

Condolences for taking Math 3A over the summer. I also did and while the class was pretty easy, summer school just blows.

Top by far. Inf 43 is free, GEs tend to be free. 46 is very hard, but 53 is probably harder, and if you have Wongma it is absolutely harder, or at least higher workload. Taking a light load with data structures is nice since it lets you focus on it.

Winter 2023:

Winter 2023:

Assuming Wongma 51, about the same probably. 51 from what I've heard is higher workload than 46 (and having taken Wongma 51 I can guarantee its a massive workload, though a fantastic class and imo worth). Assuming anyone else, the top is much easier.

Pretty similar, its hard to plan that far in advance anyway but 161/53 are both hard, 171 I've hard is easier.

I'd go for the schedule on top or 46/51/INF43 next fall to make your Winter 2023 really chill.

I'd go for the schedule on top or 46/51/INF43 next fall to make your Winter 2023 really chill.

Another thing to keep in mind, you don't have to finish 51/53 before starting Upper Divs (some of which are actually easier). Check the prereqs for the UD class you're interested in, and you should be able to enroll. (e.g. the prereq for CS 125 is only ICS 33, so you can jump straight into that without 45C/46/51/53).

So next fall you can do: ICS 46, INF 43, GE.

Then Winter: ICS 139W, Anthro 2A, [another low workload class]. If you have a specialization as a CS major, there's a list of accepted INF classes on the course catalogue that count towards your requirements. There's also some lower-workload UD CS classes depending on who is teaching that quarter.

Spring: ICS 51, CS 161, [another CS class]

You can also consider part-time status or withdrawing for Winter 2023. If your commitment is an internship, something employment related, something health related, etc you can apply for part-time status for Winter 2023. And you can take up to 10 units (you can do even less if you'd like) at a reduced tuition price. It's pretty easy to get granted part-time status from ICS with documentation. Also, if it doesn't interfere with your housing situation/graduation date, you could apply to withdraw for just Winter 2023 if you want to focus on just your commitment.

If the professor for 51 is Wong-ma, it's a very heavy workload. She's a phenomenal professor but it's a 6 unit class that feels like 8 or 10. If the professor is Harris, it's the same-ish projects with bad lectures lol

I personally found 46 easy-ish with Shindler (he kinda goes off topic though), but it's a hard class for some people. The projects took me 3-8 hrs, and you can just copy from the textbook pseudocode.

Hello, I have to complete the following eight lower divs for a CS major:

ICS 51, ICS 45c, ICS 6D, ICS 46, ICS 53, ICS 53L, Stats 67, IN4MTX 43

I already finished all my math courses, GEs and other lower div CS at community college, so my courseload is going to only these CS courses, which I know will be tough. How would you rank the difficulty of these courses, from easiest to hardest? If I wanted to take all of these courses in 2-3 quarters, how would I divide it evenly?

Thanks! Your advice is much appreciated :)

Fall: ICS 45c, ICS 51, Inf 43.

Winter: ICS 46, ICS 6D

Spring: ICS 53, Stats 67 edit - realistically id take cs 161 instead of 53

From the course I would say 53 has the highest workload with 43 being the least and 67 as well. So I would take those together. If you take 53 though, take it with Wongma unless you’re already familiar with C and don’t mind disorganized class with Harris. Honestly I’d avoid Harris since people still have grade problems from what I heard even after the quarter has been over. I also heard he coast after lectures so no commutations on piazza, emails etc. People were pretty frustrated I’m sure. They didn’t have an autograder either so a whole bunch of problems with submissions. I will say that wongma is one of the professor you need to take if you’re at uci. She will give you everything you need to do well in the class as well as learn. The first couple weeks may be strenuous but you will get
to do well in the class as well as learn. The first couple weeks may be strenuous but you will get used to it and it will mellow out as well.

I agree with tippitytoppitytoop about 53 having the highest workload and needing to be familiar with C before taking it. I personally found taking 45C before 53 was enough for me to get by.

Additionally To perhaps offer additonal perspective about deciding who to take 53 with, I took 53 with both Wongma and Harris. The first was with Wongma and the second time was with Harris. I don't recall having any issues with grades with Harris nor did I think the class was disorganized at the time.

There is no doubt that Wongma's classes are more organized and structured. She definitely spends more time going into details about how things work. This can be good thing for those who want to understand, but at the same time a burden as it felt like the work was more difficult because she expected everyone to put in the time to understand. Harris on the other hand felt more straight forward in that he provided the basic notes required to get started on the assignments. That being said while taking both courses I still had to do additional research on my own to understand the assignments.

I think its also important to say upon reviewing my old notes, both Wongma and Harris utilize the same core notes from Carnegie Mellon. While Harris just went over them essentially just reading the slides with occasional insight; Wongma on the otherhand at times would spend additional time working through examples or have additional notes that would help with understanding. Lastly, something that I distinctly remember was with Wongma we always had to declare variables dynamically whenever we could as opposed to with Harris we could statically declare everything at the beginning.

Feel free to dm me if you want to know any more specifics between the two.

Taking 46 and 53 in one quarter is gonna be really rough, especially if you’re taking Wong for 53. So with that in mind, I say just take 43 in that quarter as it’s the easiest class in that bunch. If you’re trying to do everything in 2 quarters, I say 51, 45C, 6D and 67 for fall, 46, 53, 43 for winter. Honestly it depends on how strong your math skills are bc if you’re not too great at math, then 6D and 67 are gonna take a lot of your time. 51 is okay, it was more boring for me since that’s when you learn about assembly level shit, and 45c is def not a free class, but it’s nothing crazy either. 46, 53, 43 tho, def I would recommend separating 46 and 53 if you can, esp bc 46 is such an important class and I argue that it’s better to just space it out so u can actually focus in that class

class and I argue that it’s better to just space it out so u can actually focus in that class versus struggling to pass with a C just bc you wanna finish your lower divs asap. But workload wise, as long as your on top of your shit you’ll be fine

Suggestion:

Fall: ICS 51, IN4MTX 43, ICS 6D or Stats 67

Winter: ICS 45C, ICS 53 and 53L (taken together),

Spring: ICS 46, ICS 6D or Stat 67 (whichever you didn't take in Fall, obviously)

You might want to move 6D/67 to Spring from Winter (not Fall), depending on what upper division you want and if it's available in Winter or Spring.
